item.title: Beijing Warns EU After China Firms Included in Russia Sanctions
item.description: China said it will take necessary measures after the European Union added Chinese entities to its latest Russia sanctions package, warning that all consequences would be borne by the EU.

item.title: Trump’s Hormuz Blockade Has Deepened a Historic Shipping Crisis
item.description: In early April — after a month of disruption around one of the world’s most important energy chokepoints — US President Donald Trump wrote that with “a little ​more time, we can easily OPEN THE ​HORMUZ STRAIT, TAKE THE OIL, & MAKE A ⁠FORTUNE.”

item.title: Fed Set to Lead Uneasy G-7 With Rates Kept on Hold This Week
item.description: Policymakers in the US and across the Group of Seven will probably keep interest rates steady this week while watching nervously for signs of higher energy costs fanning inflation.

item.title: Hungary’s Next Premier Warns Investors to Shun Orban-Tied Assets
item.description: Incoming Hungarian Prime Minister Peter Magyar said he had information that wealthy figures linked to Viktor Orban’s outgoing government were moving assets abroad and called on authorities to detain fleeing “oligarch” families.

item.title: Cheap Private Credit Funds Draw Bargain Hunters
item.description: Bargain hunters are snatching up private lending funds in the stock market, eager to buy assets that look cheap by a key measure.

item.title: Macron Says Would Be ‘Silly’ Not to Roll Over EU Covid-Era Debt
item.description: French President Emmanuel Macron said the European Union’s Covid-era debt should be rolled over and the bloc should issue new bonds, making the case for flexibility during “extraordinary times.”

item.title: Private-Sector Sleuthing Becomes Big Business for US Tech Startup
item.description: Trump’s economic crackdown on China’s US interests is creating opportunities for a Utah-based intelligence firm

item.title: Hormuz Crisis Is ‘Biggest Energy Disruption Ever,’ Yergin Says
item.description: S&P Global Vice Chairman Daniel Yergin warned that the Strait of Hormuz crisis represents “the biggest energy disruption we’ve ever seen,” despite oil prices not yet reaching the inflation-adjusted equivalent of previous highs.

item.title: ‘He Has the Market in a Chokehold’: Stocks Swing as Trump Posts
item.description: There are lots of things that can move the stock market, from economic data, to Federal Reserve pronouncements, to corporate developments. But for the past 15 months, traders’ fortunes have been largely tethered to the whims of a single person: President Donald Trump.

item.title: Bitcoin’s Stealth Rally Has Traders Setting Sights on $80,000
item.description: Bitcoin is approaching $80,000 for the first time since January — a stealth recovery built not on euphoria but on short covering and the relentless accumulation by one company: Strategy Inc.

item.title: World’s Oldest Financial Institutions Facing New Levels of Risk
item.description: The IMF is more important than ever in times of crisis

item.title: Strait of Hormuz Remains Near-Empty With Just a Few Iran Ships Moving
item.description: The Strait of Hormuz remains largely empty of merchant ships, with only a few Tehran-linked vessels moving through the waterway, following a tense week that saw Iranian gunboat attacks and tanker interceptions by the US Navy.

item.title: The Hormuz Billion-Barrel Oil Shock Is About to Crash Demand
item.description: The Strait of Hormuz oil shock has yet to crash demand as the rich world borrows from its stocks and pays up to secure supply. Traders are now sounding the alarm that a harsh adjustment is coming.

item.title: What It Means for South America to Finally Enjoy ‘Normal Recessions’
item.description: Amid Iran-war upheaval, some emerging markets are doing just fine.

item.title: China Warns of Heavy Rain, Flood Risks Ahead of May Day Holiday
item.description: Chinese weather authorities warned of heavy rainfall across southern regions from April 26 to April 29, with downpours expected to hit areas already saturated by earlier storms, raising the risk of flooding and landslides.

item.title: Thousands Protest Against Outgoing Malaysian Anti-Graft Chief
item.description: Thousands of Malaysians gathered in Kuala Lumpur on Saturday, reiterating calls for the establishment of a Royal Commission of Inquiry into allegations involving outgoing anti-graft chief Azam Baki.

item.title: Charting the Global Economy: Euro Area Business Activity Sags
item.description: Business activity in the euro area contracted for the first time since 2024 on a pronounced weakening in the services sector as war in the Middle East suppressed demand.

item.title: Understanding the Most Viral Chart in Artificial Intelligence
item.description: Another chart that goes up and to the right.

item.title: Odd Lots: Understanding the Most Viral Chart in AI (Podcast)
item.description: Odd Lots: Understanding the Most Viral Chart in AI (Podcast)【原文：Odd Lots: Understanding the Most Viral Chart in AI (Podcast)】

item.title: Russian Attack on Ukrainian Cities Kills Four People, Zelenskiy Says
item.description: Russia carried out one of the largest attacks on Ukraine overnight, killing four people and leaving over 30 wounded across the country, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Telegram.

item.title: Merz’s Big Year of Economic Renewal in Germany Is Going Awry
item.description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz’s bid for a meaningful rebound in 2026 is getting harder to deliver by the week, despite the huge stimulus flowing into Europe’s biggest economy.

item.title: Graticule’s Levinson ‘Open’ to Iran Resolution Ahead of Rally
item.description: Investor Adam Levinson said he remains bullish despite recent market volatility, and is buying the dip ahead of an expected rally on a resolution in the Iran conflict.

item.title: PBOC Warns of Finance ‘Involution,’ Reaffirms Risk Control Focus
item.description: China’s central bank and foreign‑exchange regulator reiterated the need to curb excessive — or “involution‑style” — competition within the country’s finance industry, framing it as a governance and discipline concern.

item.title: US Grants Maduro Access to Funds for Defense in Criminal Case
item.description: US officials reversed themselves and will now allow Nicolás Maduro and his wife to pay lawyers with Venezuelan funds as they defend themselves in the drug-trafficking case against them in New York.

item.title: Xi Calls Laos Key Priority in China’s Neighborhood Diplomacy
item.description: Chinese President Xi Jinping said that Beijing has always viewed its relations with Laos from a strategic and long-term perspective, describing the country as a key priority in China’s diplomacy with neighboring nations.

item.title: China Says US Export Bills Risk Disrupting Chip Supply Chains
item.description: China said it is closely monitoring US legislative plans after the House Foreign Affairs Committee advanced export-control bills targeting semiconductors, which Beijing warned could disrupt global supply chains if passed.

item.title: Malaysia Picks Abdul Halim Bin Aman as Its New Anti-Graft Chief
item.description: Malaysia named Abdul Halim Bin Aman as the nation’s next anti-graft chief, taking over from current head Azam Baki when his term ends next month.

item.title: Denso to Withdraw Proposal to Take Over Rohm, Nikkei Says
item.description: Denso Corp. is set to withdraw its proposal to take over semiconductor maker Rohm Co., as the two sides were unable to see eye to eye on an offer, Nikkei reported.

item.title: Oracle Data Center $16 Billion Financing Gets Over The Line
item.description: A $16 billion financing for a giant Oracle Corp. data center in Michigan has wrapped after months of stop-and-start negotiations with investors.

item.title: AI Chip Surge Elevates Taiwan, Korea in Global Equity Rankings
item.description: The artificial intelligence boom has triggered a seismic reshuffling of global equity markets, with Taiwan and South Korea muscling past European nations one by one.
